message.thinking:
We need to produce JSON with fields. Need to summarize the post in 55 words or fewer, one or two short sentences. Must preserve stance, uncertainty, qualification, conversational intent. Provide phrase candidates: 1-3 short reusable phrase candidates copied exactly from authored_text. Must be substrings exactly. Avoid markdown links unless necessary. Prefer different phrase without markdown link. Must not invent facts. Provide important_uncertainty: a string or null. Provide conversational_intent: e.g., "questioning and evaluating" or "seeking feedback" etc. Stance: maybe "questioning" or "qualifying" or "seeking feedback". Technical burden: low/medium/high. Phrase learning potential: high/medium/low. Provide phrase candidates: each with text and function. Must be substrings from authored_text. Let's find suitable substrings.

Authored_text: 
"The goal with these non-standard targets isn't for them to be installed (by `pip` or related). They exist for any other context in which python code will need to be distributed. I.e. `shiv` or the other examples given earlier.

I understand that I'm approaching this from a more abstract standpoint as opposed to addressing a concrete problem.

I can't definitively say this one way of the other, but since this ecosystem is still relatively new, I feel as though the users who need to create custom targets have probably just stayed with their old build system.

Since I only provided examples for hatch targets in the original post, I went to go look for projects using distutils/[`setuptools.Command`](https://setuptools.pypa.io/en/latest/userguide/extension.html#setuptools.Command) to create these custom targets and found these.

- [py2app](https://github.com/ronaldoussoren/py2app/tree/main): creating macOS `.app` files of executeable python.

- [clamped](https://github.com/jimbaker/clamped): converting python code to java for use with Jython

- [pex](https://pex.readthedocs.io/en/v2.1.41/buildingpex.html#using-bdist-pex): pex uses `bdist_pex` to build `.pex` files with setuptools

- [conda-build](https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda-build/en/24.3.x/user-guide/recipes/build-without-recipe.html), [hatch-conda-build](https://pypi.org/project/hatch-conda-build/): builds conda packages

- [cx-freeze](https://cx-freeze.readthedocs.io/en/stable/builtdist.html): pretty much just `py2app` for multiple OSes

(side note: I found [Nuitka](https://nuitka.net/user-documentation/use-cases.html#setuptools-wheels) which compiles python into bytecode, but still seems to create a `.whl`, so that wouldn't really be considered a different target. It does create non wheel targets, so this proposal might be helpful for it, but it doesn't seem to use the traditional python packaging tools. I didn't dive deep enough to see how dependency management and environment creation is handled in the build process)

To answer the “what frontends do I expect to use this” more directly, I can see uv, hatch, pdm, and other frontends that strive to be extensible providing support for this (once again, hatch already supports this. Standardization of this interface would allow using the uv frontend with the hatchling backend). I am of the mentality that providing extensibility to python developers is good, but if it seems like this will go unused, then I can understand the desire to keep things simple.

Maybe I'm demanding too much within the constraints of the build ecosystem and instead this sort of process should operate on already built `.whl`s like how [PyApp](https://github.com/ofek/pyapp) works, but I find it convenient for developers to just have frontend tools like uv that are able to handle all parts of taking python code from its source to its final deliverable.

I would love to get some feedback from any of these frontend developers if this is something they can see being standardized."

We need phrase candidates: maybe "I can't definitively say this one way or the other" but that phrase is not exact; the text says "I can't definitively say this one way of the other". The phrase "I can't definitively say this one way of the other" is exact. Let's check: The text: "I can't definitively say this one way of the other, but since this ecosystem is still relatively new, I feel as though the users who need to create custom targets have probably just stayed with their old build system." So phrase candidate: "I can't definitively say this one way of the other". That is exact substring. Good.

Another phrase: "I would love to get some feedback from any of these frontend developers if this is something they can see being standardized." That's exact. Good.

Another phrase: "I am of the mentality that providing extensibility to python developers is good" maybe. But we need 1-3 candidates. Let's pick two or three.

Candidate 1: "I can't definitively say this one way of the other" function: expressing uncertainty.

Candidate 2: "I would love to get some feedback from any of these frontend developers if this is something they can see being standardized." function: seeking feedback.

Candidate 3: maybe "I am of the mentality that providing extensibility to python developers is good" function: expressing stance.

But we need to keep phrase short. The third phrase is long but still okay. But we can choose two candidates to keep simple. The requirement: 1-3 candidates. Let's choose 2.

Now summary: 55 words or fewer. One or two short natural English sentences. Must preserve stance, uncertainty, qualification, conversational intent. The post is about standardizing build backend support for non-standard targets. The author says goal is not for them to be installed, but for distribution contexts. They are uncertain about adoption, mention that users may stay with old build system. They list examples. They say they can see frontends that support it, but if unused they'd keep simple. They ask for feedback.

So summary: "I’m exploring whether non‑standard build targets should be standardized, noting that many developers still use older systems and that adoption by frontends like uv, hatch, and pdm is uncertain. I’d appreciate feedback from frontend developers on whether this standardization would be useful." That's 44 words. Good.

Now conversational_intent: "seeking feedback" or "questioning and evaluating". The author is asking for feedback. So "seeking feedback" is appropriate.

Stance: "questioning" or "qualifying". The author expresses uncertainty. So stance: "questioning".

Technical burden: low. The author is not providing code, just discussing. So low.

Phrase learning potential: high. The phrases are reusable.

Important uncertainty: "I can't definitively say this one way of the other" expresses uncertainty. So we can set important_uncertainty to that phrase. Or we can set to a string summarizing: "I can't definitively say this one way of the other". That is the phrase. Good.

Now produce JSON.

Check schema: all required fields present. phrase_candidates array with objects each having text and function. Ensure no additional properties.

Let's craft.
